summary: Patrick McKenzie wrote a parody rap (to the tune of Eminem's 'The Real Slim Shady') celebrating solo software founders who build and market products without cofounders or VC funding. The song pushes back against Silicon Valley orthodoxy that startups require teams, funding, and exits to succeed. He argues that solo developers can handle both coding and marketing, that niche products with paying customers are legitimate businesses, and that profit can beat exits. The post also casually reveals he has quit his day job to go full-time on his software business. It's a humorous manifesto for the bootstrapped, one-person software company.
key_points: Solo founders can successfully handle both engineering and marketing — the 'engineers can't market' narrative is false
Niche software products with paying customers and high margins are legitimate businesses, even if they aren't glamorous
The VC-funded, team-based startup model is not the only path — profitability can beat chasing exits
McKenzie quietly reveals he has quit his day job to work on his software business full-time
The enormous addressable market of non-technical users is underserved by developers who only build for other developers
money_quotes: Sometimes a profit beats an exit.
Try selling to women -- you'll be swimmin' in it.
My customers can't even spell PHP. So what? Their money's green as your ping stats.
And if we can grok code, AdWords, and email, then there's no reason we can't do software retail.
Every single one of y'all could try it.
key_insight: Solo founders who combine technical skills with marketing ability can build profitable software businesses without cofounders, funding, or Silicon Valley's permission.
